schools.summary: The LORD visits Sarah: 'and the LORD visited Sarah as he had said, and the LORD did unto Sarah as he had spoken' — God visiting Sarah (in mercy, to give her the promised son, the fulfilling of the promise, 17:21; 18:10, 14, the LORD doing exactly as he had said, the faithfulness of God in keeping his word); the LORD 'visited Sarah' (God's gracious regard to the barren matriarch, 11:30, the visitation of mercy, enabling her to conceive, against nature and age, 18:11, God's power and faithfulness displayed in the once-barren, now-fruitful Sarah); the double 'as he had said... as he had spoken' emphasizing that God did exactly what he had promised (the certainty and faithfulness of God's word, the promise, long delayed, 12:2; 15:4; 17:16; 18:10, now fulfilled to the letter, God's faithfulness to his covenant) — Ellicott, Gill, Calvin, Henry.

themes: And the LORD visited Sarah as he had said, and the LORD did unto Sarah as he had spoken
the LORD visiting Sarah, fulfilling his promise
God's gracious visit to the barren matriarch (11:30)
the faithfulness of God, fulfilling the long-delayed promise to the letter (18:14)

schools.summary: Sarah bears Isaac at the set time: 'for Sarah conceived, and bare Abraham a son in his old age, at the set time of which God had spoken to him' — Sarah, barren (11:30) and past age (18:11), conceiving and bearing a son in Abraham's old age (he being a hundred, v.5, the miraculous birth, against nature, by God's power, 18:14; Romans 4:19-21; Hebrews 11:11, the son of promise born to the aged parents); the son born 'in his old age' (to a hundred-year-old father and a ninety-year-old mother, 17:17, the human impossibility overcome by God's power, the child of the promise, given against nature, magnifying God's faithfulness and might); 'at the set time of which God had spoken to him' (Isaac born at the very time God had appointed, 17:21; 18:14, 'at this set time in the next year,' the precise fulfillment of the dated promise, God's faithfulness to the appointed time, the son of promise born exactly as and when God had said) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, Calvin, Wesley.

themes: For Sarah conceived, and bare Abraham a son in his old age, at the set time of which God had spoken to him
Sarah conceiving and bearing Isaac at the appointed time
the miraculous birth against nature, by God's power (Hebrews 11:11)
the precise fulfillment of the dated promise (17:21; 18:14)

schools.summary: Abraham names Isaac: 'and Abraham called the name of his son that was born unto him, whom Sarah bare to him, Isaac' — Abraham naming the child Isaac ('he laughs/laughter'), as God had directed (17:19, the name commemorating the laughter — Abraham's joyful laugh, 17:17, Sarah's, 18:12; 21:6, and the joy of the long-awaited son, the son of laughter named according to God's word); the son the one 'whom Sarah bare to him' (the child of Sarah, the wife, the matriarch of the promise, not Hagar, the son of the free, the covenant-seed, 17:19, 21, the distinction marking Isaac as the son of promise); 'Isaac (laughter)' (the name given by God before the birth, 17:19, the son of joy and laughter, the name a perpetual memorial of the wonder and joy of his birth, and of the parents' laughter, of faith and joy, the child of the promise) — Ellicott, Gill, JFB, Calvin.

themes: And Abraham called the name of his son that was born unto him, whom Sarah bare to him, Isaac
Abraham naming his son Isaac
the son named 'laughter' by God's direction (17:19), the son of joy
the son of Sarah, the covenant-seed of promise

schools.summary: Abraham circumcises Isaac: 'and Abraham circumcised his son Isaac being eight days old, as God had commanded him' — Abraham circumcising Isaac on the eighth day (17:12), in obedience to God's command (17:10-14, the covenant-sign administered to the son of promise, the patriarch's exact obedience to the covenant ordinance); Isaac circumcised on the eighth day (the appointed time, 17:12, later the day of naming, Luke 2:21, the covenant-sign borne by the son of promise at the appointed time, the obedience to the ordinance); 'as God had commanded him' (Abraham's exact, prompt obedience to the command of circumcision, 17:10-14, the son of promise brought into the covenant by the sign, the patriarch faithful to the covenant ordinance, the obedience of faith) — Gill, Clarke, Calvin.

themes: And Abraham circumcised his son Isaac being eight days old, as God had commanded him
Abraham circumcising Isaac on the eighth day
the son of promise brought into the covenant by the sign at the appointed time (17:12)
the patriarch's exact obedience of faith

schools.summary: Abraham's age at Isaac's birth: 'and Abraham was an hundred years old, when his son Isaac was born unto him' — Abraham a hundred years old at Isaac's birth (and Sarah ninety, 17:17, the great age of the parents marking the miraculous nature of the birth, the son of promise born to the centenarian father, the human impossibility, Romans 4:19, overcome by God's power); Abraham's hundred years (and Sarah's ninety) making the birth humanly impossible (the child given against nature, the age magnifying God's faithfulness and might, the son of promise born when the parents were as good as dead, Hebrews 11:11-12, Romans 4:19); Abraham at a hundred (twenty-five years after his call at seventy-five, 12:4, one year after the covenant of circumcision at ninety-nine, 17:1, 24) having the son of promise (the long trial of faith ended in the fulfillment, the dating marking the patience of faith rewarded, the son given at the appointed time, v.2) — Gill, Calvin.

themes: And Abraham was an hundred years old, when his son Isaac was born unto him
Abraham's age at Isaac's birth
the great age marking the miraculous birth (Romans 4:19; Hebrews 11:11-12)
the long trial of faith ended in the fulfillment

schools.summary: Sarah's laughter of joy: 'and Sarah said, God hath made me to laugh, so that all that hear will laugh with me' — 'God hath made me to laugh' Sarah's laughter now the laughter of joy and praise, in contrast to her former laughter of unbelief and incredulity (18:12-15, God having turned her doubting laugh into a laugh of joy at the birth of Isaac, whose name is 'laughter,' the once-barren matriarch rejoicing in God's gift, the laughter of faith and gladness, not of doubt, Clarke noting the word 'to laugh' here not in the sense of derision but of joy); 'all that hear will laugh with me' (all who heard of the wonder rejoicing with Sarah, sharing her joy at the marvelous birth, the dominant reading — laughing with her in joy and congratulation, some noting a possible secondary sense, that some might laugh at her, the old woman with a child, but the joyful reading prevailing, the wonder calling forth joy and praise from all who heard, the shared laughter of joy at God's gift); Sarah's laugh now, of joy, set against her former laugh, of unbelief, 18:12 (the change wrought by God's faithfulness, the name Isaac, 'laughter,' commemorating the joy, the matriarch's praise) — Clarke, Gill.

debates.question: What is Sarah's laughter now, and what is 'laugh with me'?

sides.position: The laughter of joy and praise, in contrast to her former unbelieving laugh (18:12) - God turning her doubt to gladness; 'laugh with me' = rejoice with her (the dominant reading)
A possible secondary hint that some might laugh at her (the old woman with a child), though the joyful reading prevails

themes: And Sarah said, God hath made me to laugh, so that all that hear will laugh with me
Sarah's laughter of joy (the crux of her changed laughter)
the laughter of joy and praise, in contrast to her former unbelieving laugh (18:12)
'laugh with me' = rejoice with her at God's gift

schools.summary: Sarah marvels at the wonder: 'and she said, Who would have said unto Abraham, that Sarah should have given children suck? for I have born him a son in his old age' — Sarah marveling at the unlooked-for wonder: who could have foretold that she (barren, aged) would nurse a child? (the astonishment at God's gift, the once-barren matriarch now a nursing mother, the wonder of the miraculous birth); Sarah herself nursing Isaac ('given children suck') a token of the completeness of the miracle (not only conceiving and bearing, but nursing, in her old age, the matriarch's own care of the son of promise, the wonder magnified); 'I have born him a son in his old age' (Sarah's praise and wonder at bearing a son to Abraham in his, and her, old age, the acknowledgment of God's power and faithfulness, the joy of the long-awaited son, the matriarch's grateful astonishment at the fulfillment of the promise) — Gill, Calvin.

themes: And she said, Who would have said unto Abraham, that Sarah should have given children suck? for I have born him a son in his old age
Sarah marveling at the wonder of bearing a son in old age
the once-barren matriarch now a nursing mother, the miracle complete
the grateful astonishment and praise at the fulfillment of the promise

schools.summary: Isaac's weaning and the feast: 'and the child grew, and was weaned: and Abraham made a great feast the same day that Isaac was weaned' — Isaac growing and being weaned (in the East, often at two or three years of age, Abraham making a great feast to mark the weaning, a customary celebration, the joy of the household at the thriving of the son of promise, the feast of thanksgiving); Isaac growing and reaching weaning — a significant milestone (the child past the dangers of infancy, the occasion for the feast, the thriving of the son of promise under God's blessing); 'a great feast' (Abraham celebrating the weaning with a great feast, a joyful family occasion, the gratitude and joy at the son of promise, yet the feast becoming the occasion of the incident with Ishmael, v.9, the celebration that set the stage for the casting out of Hagar and Ishmael) — Ellicott, Gill, Calvin.

themes: And the child grew, and was weaned: and Abraham made a great feast the same day that Isaac was weaned
Isaac's weaning and Abraham's feast
the joy of the household at the thriving of the son of promise
the feast that became the occasion of the incident with Ishmael

schools.summary: Ishmael mocking: 'and Sarah saw the son of Hagar the Egyptian, which she had born unto Abraham, mocking' — Henry and the dominant reading (with Galatians 4:29) holding Ishmael's 'mocking' a mocking and persecuting of Isaac: Ishmael (then about sixteen or seventeen) mocking, scoffing at, or deriding Isaac (the heir of promise), perhaps in envy and scorn at the feast (v.8), or persecuting him, Paul interpreting it as persecution — 'he that was born after the flesh persecuted him that was born after the Spirit' (Galatians 4:29), the mocking a real offense, a scorning of the child of promise (and, some hold, mixed with profanity or idolatry), the carnal scoffing at the spiritual, a great sin; a milder reading taking the 'mocking' (the Hebrew metsacheq, a play on Isaac's name, 'laughing/playing') as mere playing or laughing (Ishmael playing, perhaps boisterously, or in a way that displeased Sarah, without necessarily malicious intent), but the dominant view, with Galatians 4:29 and the gravity of Sarah's response, v.10, taking it as mocking and persecuting; Sarah seeing 'the son of Hagar the Egyptian' (not 'Ishmael,' but pointedly the son of the bondwoman, the Egyptian, the contrast between the son of the bondwoman, after the flesh, and Isaac the son of the free, after the promise, the offense that prompted Sarah's demand, v.10, the distinction Paul would draw, Galatians 4:22-31) — Henry, Keil, Gill, Calvin.

sides.position: Mocking and persecuting Isaac - a scorning of the child of promise, the carnal seed against the spiritual (the dominant reading, with Galatians 4:29, 'persecuted him')
Mere playing/laughing (the wordplay on Isaac's name, metsacheq), without necessarily malicious intent

themes: And Sarah saw the son of Hagar the Egyptian, which she had born unto Abraham, mocking
Ishmael's mocking (the crux of the offense)
mocking and persecuting Isaac (Galatians 4:29) vs mere playing/laughing
the carnal seed scorning the child of promise, the act that prompted the casting out

schools.summary: Sarah demands the casting out: 'wherefore she said unto Abraham, Cast out this bondwoman and her son: for the son of this bondwoman shall not be heir with my son, even with Isaac' — Sarah demanding that Hagar (the bondwoman) and Ishmael be cast out (sent away from the household, the demand prompted by Ishmael's mocking, v.9, the separation of the bondwoman and her son from the heir of promise); Sarah's ground 'the son of this bondwoman shall not be heir with my son, even with Isaac' (Ishmael, the son of the bondwoman, must not share the inheritance with Isaac, the son of promise, the distinction between the heir of promise and the son of the flesh, the concern that the covenant-inheritance pass to Isaac alone, 17:19, 21); Sarah's demand, and God's confirming it (v.12), taken up by Paul (Galatians 4:30, 'Cast out the bondwoman and her son') in his allegory of the two covenants — Hagar/Ishmael the bondwoman and her son (the covenant of bondage, the law, the children after the flesh), Sarah/Isaac the free woman and her son (the covenant of promise, the children of the free, of grace), the casting out of the bondwoman a figure of the rejection of the covenant of works/bondage in favor of the covenant of promise (Galatians 4:21-31) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, Calvin, Wesley.

themes: Wherefore she said unto Abraham, Cast out this bondwoman and her son: for the son of this bondwoman shall not be heir with my son, even with Isaac
Sarah's demand to cast out Hagar and Ishmael
the son of the flesh distinguished from the heir of promise (17:19)
Paul's allegory of the two covenants - the bondwoman and the free (Galatians 4:21-31)

schools.summary: Abraham's grief over the demand: 'and the thing was very grievous in Abraham's sight because of his son' — Sarah's demand to cast out Hagar and Ishmael very grievous (painful, distressing) to Abraham (the patriarch's grief, chiefly 'because of his son' Ishmael, whom he loved, 17:18, the father's distress at the thought of sending away his son, the natural affection of Abraham); the demand grieving Abraham — torn between Sarah's word and his love for Ishmael (the conflict between his affection and the necessity, the distress of the father, the trial of Abraham's heart); 'because of his son' (Abraham's grief on account of Ishmael, his son, his firstborn, whom he had prayed for, 17:18, the love of the father for the child, the natural reluctance to cast him out, the affection that God's word, v.12, would overrule and comfort) — Ellicott, Gill, Calvin, Wesley.

themes: And the thing was very grievous in Abraham's sight because of his son
the grief of Abraham over Sarah's demand
the patriarch torn between Sarah's word and his love for Ishmael (17:18)
the father's distress, which God's word would overrule and comfort

schools.summary: In Isaac shall thy seed be called: 'and God said unto Abraham, Let it not be grievous in thy sight because of the lad, and because of thy bondwoman; in all that Sarah hath said unto thee, hearken unto her voice; for in Isaac shall thy seed be called' — God comforting Abraham and bidding him do as Sarah had said (cast out Hagar and Ishmael, the demand, though grievous, confirmed by God, the patriarch to obey, his grief overruled by God's word, God approving Sarah's demand for his own purpose); 'in Isaac shall thy seed be called' — the covenant-seed (the line of promise, and the seed in which all nations would be blessed, 12:3) reckoned through Isaac, not Ishmael (the election of Isaac as the line of promise, Paul citing this, Romans 9:7, 'in Isaac shall thy seed be called,' to show that 'they which are the children of the flesh, these are not the children of God: but the children of the promise are counted for the seed' — the election of grace, the seed-line not by natural descent but by promise, Hebrews 11:18 also citing it, Isaac the type and head of the children of promise, the casting out of Ishmael marking the distinction between the children of the flesh and the children of promise, Galatians 4:22-31); the line through which the promised seed (and the Messiah, Christ) would come fixed in Isaac (the covenant established with Isaac, 17:19, 21, the children of promise, of whom Isaac is the head, reckoned not by the flesh but by God's promise and election) — Ellicott, Gill, Calvin.

debates.question: What does 'in Isaac shall thy seed be called' establish?

sides.position: The election of the seed-line - the covenant-seed (and the Messiah) reckoned through Isaac the son of promise, not by natural descent; the children of promise, not of the flesh, counted for the seed (Romans 9:7-8; Hebrews 11:18), the election of grace

themes: And God said unto Abraham... in all that Sarah hath said unto thee, hearken unto her voice; for in Isaac shall thy seed be called
God's word, 'in Isaac shall thy seed be called' (the crux of the election of the seed-line)
the covenant-seed reckoned through Isaac the son of promise, not the flesh
the children of promise counted for the seed (Romans 9:7-8; Hebrews 11:18), the election of grace

schools.summary: God promises to make Ishmael a nation: 'and also of the son of the bondwoman will I make a nation, because he is thy seed' — God comforting Abraham (grieved for Ishmael, v.11) with the promise that Ishmael also (the son of the bondwoman) would be made a nation (the blessing on Ishmael, 16:10; 17:20, twelve princes, a great nation, the assurance that, though not the heir of promise, Ishmael would not be unblessed); God would make Ishmael 'a nation' (a great people, the Arab nations, 25:12-18, the temporal blessing on Ishmael for Abraham's sake, the kindness of God to the father's son, easing his grief at the parting); 'because he is thy seed' (Ishmael, being Abraham's son, seed, blessed for Abraham's sake, the regard of God to the patriarch's offspring, though the covenant-line was through Isaac, v.12, Ishmael, as Abraham's seed, becoming a nation, the comfort to the grieving father) — Ellicott, Gill, Calvin, Wesley.

themes: And also of the son of the bondwoman will I make a nation, because he is thy seed
God's promise to make Ishmael also a nation
Ishmael, though not the heir of promise, blessed for Abraham's sake (17:20)
the comfort that eased the grieving father

schools.summary: Abraham sends Hagar away: 'and Abraham rose up early in the morning, and took bread, and a bottle of water, and gave it unto Hagar, putting it on her shoulder, and the child, and sent her away: and she departed, and wandered in the wilderness of Beersheba' — Abraham, in prompt obedience to God's word (v.12), rising early and sending Hagar and Ishmael away with provisions (bread and a bottle/skin of water, the patriarch's obedience, though grievous, v.11, the provision he made for them, the sending away of the bondwoman and her son); Abraham putting the provisions on Hagar's shoulder, and (sending away) the child (the humble departure of Hagar with Ishmael, now a youth, the reduced state of the bondwoman, sent forth with bread and water, the parting from Abraham's house); 'she departed, and wandered in the wilderness of Beersheba' (Hagar going out and wandering, losing her way, or roaming, in the wilderness of Beersheba, the southern desert, the provisions soon spent, v.15, the distress of the wanderer, the setting for the deliverance by the angel, vv.17-19) — Ellicott, Gill, Henry, Calvin.

themes: And Abraham rose up early in the morning, and took bread, and a bottle of water, and gave it unto Hagar... and sent her away: and she departed, and wandered in the wilderness of Beersheba
Abraham sending Hagar and Ishmael away
the patriarch's prompt, grieved obedience to God's word (v.12)
the bondwoman and her son sent forth into the desert

schools.summary: The water spent, Ishmael laid under a shrub: 'and the water was spent in the bottle, and she cast the child under one of the shrubs' — the water (their only drink) running out in the wilderness, and Hagar, in desperation, laying (casting, placing) Ishmael under a shrub (a desert bush, for what little shade it gave, the extremity of their distress — water gone, the youth fainting, from thirst, perhaps ill, the mother's anguish); the spending of the water bringing them to the brink of death (the desperate condition in the desert, the trial of Hagar and Ishmael at their lowest, the setting for God's deliverance, vv.17-19); 'she cast the child under one of the shrubs' (Hagar laying Ishmael, now fainting, under a bush, unable to carry him further or bear to watch him die, the mother's despairing act, the youth left in the shade of a shrub, the picture of human extremity, on the edge of death, where God's help would come) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, Clarke, Ellicott.

themes: And the water was spent in the bottle, and she cast the child under one of the shrubs
the water spent and Hagar laying the child under a shrub
the extremity of distress in the desert, on the brink of death
the picture of human extremity where God's help would come (vv.17-19)

schools.summary: Hagar weeps for the dying child: 'and she went, and sat her down over against him a good way off, as it were a bowshot: for she said, Let me not see the death of the child. And she sat over against him, and lift up her voice, and wept' — Hagar withdrawing a little distance (a bowshot) and sitting down opposite Ishmael, unable to bear watching him die (the mother's anguish, removing herself from the sight of her child's death, the depth of her despair); Hagar saying 'Let me not see the death of the child' (her dread of watching Ishmael die, the helpless grief of the mother, the abandonment of hope, the extremity of the mother's sorrow); 'she sat over against him, and lift up her voice, and wept' (Hagar weeping aloud in her despair, the mother's tears for her dying son, the grief that God would hear and answer, v.17, 'God heard the voice of the lad,' the weeping that preceded the deliverance) — Ellicott, Gill.

themes: And she went, and sat her down over against him a good way off, as it were a bowshot: for she said, Let me not see the death of the child. And she... lift up her voice, and wept
Hagar's despair as she weeps for the dying child
the mother's anguish, unable to watch Ishmael die
the grief that God would hear and answer (v.17)

schools.summary: God hears the lad, the angel comforts Hagar: 'and God heard the voice of the lad; and the angel of God called to Hagar out of heaven, and said unto her, What aileth thee, Hagar? fear not; for God hath heard the voice of the lad where he is' — God hearing the cry of Ishmael (the lad, perhaps crying out in his distress, or his cry going up to God, fittingly, 'Ishmael' meaning 'God hears,' 16:11, the God who hears the afflicted hearing the dying youth, the deliverance prompted by God's hearing); 'the angel of God called to Hagar out of heaven... What aileth thee, Hagar? fear not' (the angel calling from heaven, asking Hagar what troubled her, and bidding her not fear, the comfort of God to the despairing mother, as the Angel had found her before, 16:7-13, the gracious word 'fear not' to the weeping Hagar); 'God hath heard the voice of the lad where he is' (God having heard Ishmael where he lay, under the shrub, v.15, the assurance that God's eye and ear were on the dying youth, the deliverance at hand, the comfort that God had not forsaken them, the regard of God to the afflicted lad) — Ellicott, Gill, Wesley, Calvin.

themes: And God heard the voice of the lad; and the angel of God called to Hagar out of heaven... fear not; for God hath heard the voice of the lad where he is
God hearing the lad and the angel comforting Hagar
the God who hears (Ishmael, 'God hears,' 16:11) regarding the dying youth
the angel's comfort 'fear not' to the despairing mother

schools.summary: The angel bids Hagar lift the lad: 'arise, lift up the lad, and hold him in thine hand; for I will make him a great nation' — the angel bidding Hagar arise, lift up Ishmael (raise and support him), and hold him (the comfort and the action commanded, the assurance, with the renewed promise that Ishmael would become a great nation, 16:10; 17:20; 21:13); Hagar to lift up and hold Ishmael (to take heart and care for him, the encouragement to the despairing mother, the action that, with God's provision, the well, v.19, would save the youth, the rousing of hope); 'I will make him a great nation' (the renewed promise that Ishmael would become a great nation, the Arab peoples, 25:12-18, the assurance that the dying youth would live and prosper, the ground of the comfort, God's purpose for Ishmael, for Abraham's sake, v.13) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, Calvin.

themes: Arise, lift up the lad, and hold him in thine hand; for I will make him a great nation
the angel bidding Hagar lift the lad, with the promise of a great nation
the despairing mother roused to care for Ishmael
the renewed promise that Ishmael would become a great nation (17:20)

schools.summary: God opens Hagar's eyes to the well: 'and God opened her eyes, and she saw a well of water; and she went, and filled the bottle with water, and gave the lad drink' — God enabling Hagar to see a well of water (whether one already there, which she had not seen in her distress, now revealed to her, or, by some, a well newly provided, the deliverance — water in the desert, God's provision for the dying, the opening of her eyes to the help at hand); 'God opened her eyes' (God directing Hagar's sight to the well, which her grief and despair had kept her from seeing, or which God now made visible, the provision God's gift, the deliverance from death, the eyes opened to the water of life, a figure, by some, of God opening the eyes to see his provision and grace); 'she filled the bottle with water, and gave the lad drink' (Hagar filling the skin and giving Ishmael drink, the youth revived, the deliverance from death by God's provision, the saving of Ishmael, the mercy of God to the afflicted) — Ellicott, Gill, Clarke, Calvin.

themes: And God opened her eyes, and she saw a well of water; and she went, and filled the bottle with water, and gave the lad drink
God opening Hagar's eyes to see the well
God revealing the water in the desert, delivering Ishmael from death
God's provision for the afflicted, the eyes opened to the help at hand

schools.summary: God with Ishmael in the wilderness: 'and God was with the lad; and he grew, and dwelt in the wilderness, and became an archer' — God's presence and blessing with Ishmael (the providence and care of God over the youth, for Abraham's sake, v.13, and by the promise, 16:10; 17:20, Ishmael, though not the heir of promise, watched over and blessed by God, the temporal favor of God on the lad); Ishmael 'grew, and dwelt in the wilderness' (he growing up and dwelling in the desert, the wild, free life of the Bedouin, fulfilling the word that he would be a wild man, 16:12, the prophecy of his character fulfilled, the youth thriving in the wilderness under God's care); 'became an archer' (Ishmael becoming an archer, a hunter, a man of the bow, the free, roving, hunting life of the desert, the character of the wild man, 16:12, and his descendants, the Arabs, the youth, delivered and blessed, growing into the man of the wilderness) — Ellicott, Gill, Calvin, Clarke.

themes: And God was with the lad; and he grew, and dwelt in the wilderness, and became an archer
God's being with Ishmael as he grew in the wilderness
the providence and care of God over the lad, for Abraham's sake (v.13)
the wild, free life fulfilling the word of 16:12

schools.summary: Ishmael in Paran with an Egyptian wife: 'and he dwelt in the wilderness of Paran: and his mother took him a wife out of the land of Egypt' — Ishmael settling in the wilderness of Paran (a desert region south of Canaan, toward Sinai/Arabia, his mother Hagar, herself an Egyptian, 16:1, taking him a wife from Egypt, the establishing of Ishmael in the desert, with an Egyptian wife, the founding of the Ishmaelite/Arab line); Paran a desert region (where Israel later wandered, Numbers 10:12, Ishmael's dwelling-place, the wild, desert home of the Ishmaelites, the region of the Arab peoples); 'his mother took him a wife out of the land of Egypt' (Hagar, an Egyptian, choosing an Egyptian wife for Ishmael, the Ishmaelite line, through this marriage, mixed with Egyptian blood, the founding of Ishmael's house, the separation from the line of promise, the wife not of Abraham's kindred, as Isaac's would be, ch. 24, Ishmael established apart from the covenant-line) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, Calvin.

themes: And he dwelt in the wilderness of Paran: and his mother took him a wife out of the land of Egypt
Ishmael dwelling in Paran and his Egyptian wife
the establishing of the Ishmaelite/Arab line in the desert
Ishmael's house founded apart from the covenant-line

schools.summary: Abimelech acknowledges God is with Abraham: 'and it came to pass at that time, that Abimelech and Phichol the chief captain of his host spake unto Abraham, saying, God is with thee in all that thou doest' — Abimelech (the king of Gerar, ch. 20) and Phichol (his army-commander) coming to Abraham to make a covenant (vv.23-32, the king seeking a treaty with the prospering patriarch, the approach of the Philistine ruler); Abimelech saying 'God is with thee in all that thou doest' (the heathen king acknowledging that God was with Abraham, prospering him in all things, the testimony of the pagan to God's blessing on the patriarch, the recognition of Abraham's favor with God, the witness of the world to the blessing on the godly); Abimelech, perceiving God's blessing on Abraham (and perhaps remembering the affair of ch. 20), seeking a covenant of peace and good faith with the prospering, God-favored patriarch (the king's prudence in securing the friendship of one so blessed, the providence that gave Abraham favor and influence among the Philistines) — Ellicott, Gill, Henry, Calvin.

themes: And it came to pass at that time, that Abimelech and Phichol the chief captain of his host spake unto Abraham, saying, God is with thee in all that thou doest
Abimelech's acknowledgment that God was with Abraham
the heathen king's testimony to God's blessing on the patriarch
the king seeking a covenant of peace with the God-favored Abraham

schools.summary: Abimelech seeks an oath of good faith: 'now therefore swear unto me here by God that thou wilt not deal falsely with me, nor with my son, nor with my son's son: but according to the kindness that I have done unto thee, thou shalt do unto me, and to the land wherein thou hast sojourned' — Abimelech asking Abraham to swear (by God) that he would deal honestly with him and his descendants (the request for a covenant of good faith, binding for the future, the king securing peace between his line and Abraham's); the oath to extend to Abimelech's posterity ('my son, my son's son') — a lasting covenant of good faith between the two houses (the king's care for his descendants, the perpetual treaty); 'according to the kindness that I have done unto thee' (Abimelech appealing to the kindness he had shown Abraham, the welcome and gifts, 20:14-15, the freedom to dwell in his land, he asking the like kindness in return, the basis of the covenant in mutual good faith, the just request of the king) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, Calvin.

themes: Now therefore swear unto me here by God that thou wilt not deal falsely with me, nor with my son, nor with my son's son... according to the kindness that I have done unto thee
Abimelech seeking an oath of good faith from Abraham
a perpetual covenant of good faith between the two houses
the appeal to the kindness shown (20:14-15)

schools.summary: Abraham consents to the oath: 'and Abraham said, I will swear' — Abraham consenting to Abimelech's request, agreeing to take the oath of good faith (the patriarch's readiness to make the covenant of peace, the agreement to deal honestly with Abimelech and his house, v.23, the consent to the treaty); Abraham agreeing to swear — to bind himself by oath to good faith (the lawful use of an oath, swearing by God, as Abimelech asked, v.23, the patriarch's willingness to secure peace, the consent of the godly man to a just covenant); Abraham's consent to the oath establishing the covenant of peace and good faith with Abimelech (the patriarch securing peace with the Philistine king, the lawful oath ratifying the treaty, the godly man dealing honestly and peaceably with his neighbor, Romans 12:18, the consent that led to the covenant, vv.27-32) — Gill, Calvin.

themes: And Abraham said, I will swear
Abraham's consent to the oath
the lawful use of an oath, the patriarch's readiness for peace
the consent establishing the covenant of good faith (Romans 12:18)

schools.summary: Abraham reproves Abimelech over the well: 'and Abraham reproved Abimelech because of a well of water, which Abimelech's servants had violently taken away' — before (or in) the covenant, Abraham reproving Abimelech over a well that the king's servants had seized by force (the patriarch's grievance about the water, a precious resource in the dry land, the honest dealing of Abraham, raising the matter that it might be set right); the well of water a valuable possession in the desert (the water Abraham had dug for, v.30, the servants' violent seizing of it a wrong, the grievance worth raising); 'which Abimelech's servants had violently taken away' (the servants, without the king's knowledge, v.26, having seized the well by force, Abraham reproving the matter, that it might be settled before the covenant, the patriarch's plainness in dealing, raising the grievance frankly, the honest, peaceable settling of the dispute, the well to be secured to Abraham by the covenant, vv.30-31) — Gill, Clarke, Calvin.

themes: And Abraham reproved Abimelech because of a well of water, which Abimelech's servants had violently taken away
Abraham reproving Abimelech over the seized well
the patriarch's honest, frank dealing, raising the grievance
the wrong to be set right and the well secured by the covenant

schools.summary: Abimelech disclaims knowledge of the well: 'and Abimelech said, I wot not who hath done this thing: neither didst thou tell me, neither yet heard I of it, but to day' — Abimelech disclaiming knowledge of the seizing of the well (he had not known who did it, nor had Abraham told him, nor had he heard of it until that day, the king's honest profession of ignorance, the wrong done by his servants without his knowledge); Abimelech professing he knew not who had taken the well (the king clearing himself of the wrong, the honest disclaimer of one who would set things right); 'neither didst thou tell me, neither yet heard I of it, but to day' (Abimelech noting that Abraham had not informed him before, nor had he heard of the matter until now, the king's clearing of himself and readiness to settle the matter, the honest dealing of Abimelech, as of Abraham, v.25, the way opened for the covenant and the securing of the well, vv.27-31) — Ellicott, Gill.

themes: And Abimelech said, I wot not who hath done this thing: neither didst thou tell me, neither yet heard I of it, but to day
Abimelech's disclaiming knowledge of the seized well
the king clearing himself of the wrong done by his servants
the honest dealing on both sides opening the way for the covenant

schools.summary: Abraham and Abimelech make a covenant: 'and Abraham took sheep and oxen, and gave them unto Abimelech; and both of them made a covenant' — Abraham giving Abimelech gifts (sheep and oxen) and the two making a covenant (a treaty of peace and good faith, the ratifying of the covenant with gifts, as a token of friendship and good will, the establishing of the treaty between the patriarch and the king); Abraham giving the gifts to Abimelech (a token of the covenant, of friendship and good faith, the seal of the treaty, the patriarch's part in establishing the peace); 'both of them made a covenant' (Abraham and Abimelech entering into a covenant, a mutual agreement of peace and good faith, ratified by oath, v.31, and gifts, the establishing of peace between the two houses, the covenant secured, and the well, vv.30-31, the godly man at peace with his neighbor, Romans 12:18, the treaty made at Beersheba, v.31) — Gill, Clarke, Calvin.

themes: And Abraham took sheep and oxen, and gave them unto Abimelech; and both of them made a covenant
Abraham and Abimelech making a covenant
the treaty of peace and good faith, ratified with gifts
the godly man at peace with his neighbor (Romans 12:18)

schools.summary: Abraham sets apart seven ewe lambs: 'and Abraham set seven ewe lambs of the flock by themselves' — Abraham setting apart seven ewe lambs from the flock (the act prompting Abimelech's question, v.29, the seven lambs to be a witness of Abraham's right to the well, v.30, the deliberate setting apart for a purpose); the seven ewe lambs (the number seven, associated with oaths and covenants — 'Beersheba,' the well of seven/of the oath, v.31) set apart (the symbolic number, the lambs to seal Abraham's claim to the well, the token of the covenant); the seven ewe lambs set apart as a witness that Abraham had dug the well (v.30, the deliberate act, to secure his right to the water by Abimelech's acceptance of the lambs, the setting that led to the naming of Beersheba, v.31, the careful establishing of his claim) — Ellicott, Gill, Clarke, Calvin.

themes: And Abraham set seven ewe lambs of the flock by themselves
Abraham setting apart seven ewe lambs
the seven lambs to be a witness of his right to the well (v.30)
the symbolic number seven, for the oath/well (Beersheba, v.31)

schools.summary: Abimelech asks the meaning of the lambs: 'and Abimelech said unto Abraham, What mean these seven ewe lambs which thou hast set by themselves?' — Abimelech, noticing the seven lambs set apart (v.28), asking their meaning (the king's question prompting Abraham's explanation, v.30, the inquiry into the purpose of the lambs); Abimelech inquiring the meaning of the seven lambs set apart (the king's curiosity, leading to Abraham's explanation, the question that opened the matter of the well, v.30); 'which thou hast set by themselves' (the lambs set apart, v.28, prompting the king's inquiry, the occasion for Abraham to declare his purpose, the witness of his right to the well, v.30, the question leading to the naming of Beersheba, v.31) — Gill, JFB.

themes: And Abimelech said unto Abraham, What mean these seven ewe lambs which thou hast set by themselves?
Abimelech asking the meaning of the seven lambs
the king's question prompting Abraham's explanation
the inquiry that opened the matter of the well (v.30)

schools.summary: Abraham explains the lambs as a witness: 'and he said, For these seven ewe lambs shalt thou take of my hand, that they may be a witness unto me, that I have digged this well' — Abraham explaining that Abimelech was to accept the seven lambs from his hand, as a witness (a public token, a legal acknowledgment) that Abraham had dug the well (and so owned it, the lambs to seal his right to the water, the securing of his claim by the king's acceptance); the lambs 'a witness' (a standing token and acknowledgment of Abraham's right to the well, the king's acceptance of them confirming the patriarch's ownership, the legal securing of the well); 'that I have digged this well' (Abraham having dug the well, the labor and the right his, the lambs witnessing his ownership against future dispute, the well having been seized before, v.25, the securing of the water for Abraham and his seed, the careful establishing of his right, leading to the naming of Beersheba, v.31) — Gill.

themes: And he said, For these seven ewe lambs shalt thou take of my hand, that they may be a witness unto me, that I have digged this well
Abraham's explanation of the seven lambs as a witness to the well
the lambs a public token of Abraham's right to the water
the securing of his claim against future dispute (v.25)

schools.summary: The naming of Beersheba: 'wherefore he called that place Beersheba; because there they sware both of them' — the place named Beersheba ('the well of the oath,' or 'the well of seven,' from the oath and the seven lambs, vv.28-30, the name commemorating the covenant and oath made there, the well and place named for the event); 'Beersheba' meaning 'the well of the oath' (sheba, oath) or 'the well of seven' (sheba, seven, from the seven lambs, the name joining the oath and the seven lambs, the memorial of the covenant, the well that would be a notable place in the patriarchal history, 26:33, the southern limit of the land, 'from Dan to Beersheba'); 'because there they sware both of them' (the name given because both Abraham and Abimelech swore there, the oath ratifying the covenant, the place of the covenant-oath, the memorial of the peace established, the well secured to Abraham by the sworn covenant) — Ellicott, Gill, Clarke, Calvin.

themes: Wherefore he called that place Beersheba; because there they sware both of them
the naming of Beersheba from the oath
Beersheba, 'the well of the oath' (or of seven), commemorating the covenant
the memorial of the peace established by the sworn covenant

schools.summary: The covenant concluded, Abimelech returns: 'thus they made a covenant at Beersheba: then Abimelech rose up, and Phichol the chief captain of his host, and they returned into the land of the Philistines' — the covenant between Abraham and Abimelech concluded at Beersheba (the king and his captain then departing, returning to their own land, the peaceful end of the meeting, the treaty established and the parties parting in peace); the covenant made at Beersheba (the treaty of peace and good faith, ratified by oath and gifts, vv.27-31, the establishing of peace between Abraham and the Philistines, the secured well and the friendship); 'they returned into the land of the Philistines' (Abimelech and Phichol going back to their land, Gerar, the Philistine country, the peaceful parting, the covenant concluded, Abraham left in possession of the well and at peace with his neighbors, the godly man dwelling in peace, Romans 12:18) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: Thus they made a covenant at Beersheba: then Abimelech rose up, and Phichol the chief captain of his host, and they returned into the land of the Philistines
the conclusion of the covenant and the return of Abimelech
the treaty of peace concluded, the parties parting in peace
Abraham left in possession of the well and at peace with his neighbors

schools.summary: Abraham plants a grove and calls on El Olam: 'and Abraham planted a grove in Beersheba, and called there on the name of the LORD, the everlasting God' — 'Abraham planted a grove (Hebrew eshel) in Beersheba' the word eshel variously rendered: a 'grove' (an orchard or plantation of trees, perhaps for shade and hospitality, a place to receive travelers and worship), or (more likely, in the modern view) a single tree, a tamarisk (a long-lived desert tree, planted as a landmark and a place of worship), some noting the later prohibition of planting groves by altars (Deuteronomy 16:21, against the heathen use of groves for idolatry), but Abraham's planting for a lawful purpose — a place of shade, hospitality, and the worship of the true God, not idolatry, the planting marking his settled dwelling and his worship at Beersheba; Abraham 'called there on the name of the LORD' (he worshiping and praying at Beersheba, as at his other dwellings, 12:8; 13:4, the public profession and worship of the true God, the patriarch's habit of worship); 'the LORD, the everlasting God (El Olam)' a title of God, 'the Everlasting' (the eternal God, who lives and reigns forever, the name marking God's eternity and unchanging faithfulness, fitting after the covenant and the long fulfillment of the promise, Abraham, the pilgrim and stranger in a land not his, calling on the everlasting God whose promises endure forever, the title of God as the Eternal One, the ground of the patriarch's faith and hope, cf. Isaiah 40:28; Romans 16:26) — Henry, Gill, Calvin, Ellicott.

debates.question: What was the 'grove' (eshel), and what is 'the everlasting God'?

sides.position: A grove/orchard of trees (for shade, hospitality, and worship), planted for a lawful purpose, not idolatry (cf. Deuteronomy 16:21); 'the everlasting God' (El Olam) the title of God as the Eternal One whose promises endure forever
A single tamarisk tree (the modern reading), planted as a landmark and place of worship; with the same title El Olam, the Eternal God, the ground of the pilgrim's faith

themes: And Abraham planted a grove in Beersheba, and called there on the name of the LORD, the everlasting God
Abraham planting a grove and calling on the everlasting God (the crux of the grove and the name)
the 'grove' (eshel) a grove/orchard or a single tamarisk, planted lawfully for worship
'the everlasting God' (El Olam), the Eternal One whose promises endure forever (Isaiah 40:28)

schools.summary: Abraham sojourns in Philistia many days: 'and Abraham sojourned in the Philistines' land many days' — Abraham dwelling as a stranger and sojourner in the land of the Philistines (Gerar, Beersheba) for a long time (the pilgrim patriarch, at peace with Abimelech, the covenant, vv.27-32, dwelling in the land, the continuance of his sojourn, the stranger and pilgrim, Hebrews 11:9, 13); Abraham 'sojourned' (dwelt as a stranger, not a permanent possessor, Acts 7:5, the pilgrim life of the patriarch, a stranger in the land of promise, the continued sojourn among the Philistines); 'many days' (Abraham dwelling there a long time, the settled, peaceful sojourn after the covenant, the patriarch at peace, worshiping the everlasting God, v.33, dwelling as a stranger in the land, the close of the chapter, before the great trial of the offering of Isaac, ch. 22) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: And Abraham sojourned in the Philistines' land many days
Abraham's long sojourn in the Philistines' land
the pilgrim patriarch, a stranger in the land of promise (Hebrews 11:9, 13; Acts 7:5)
the settled, peaceful sojourn after the covenant, before the offering of Isaac (ch. 22)
